content.text: What Pipeline Failure Triage Is

When a CI/CD pipeline for an agentic AI application fails, the raw error message rarely tells the full story. A failing eval harness stage might indicate that a prompt change degraded model quality (an eval regression), that the LLM provider's API was unavailable (an infrastructure error), that a test case itself has an ambiguous expected output (a flaky eval), or that a schema change in the eval dataset is incompatible with the scoring script. Each of these failure modes requires a different response. Pipeline failure triage is the systematic process of determining which category of failure occurred and following the corresponding runbook to resolve it.

A runbook is a documented, step-by-step playbook for responding to a specific failure category. Runbooks transform institutional knowledge—how the team has historically diagnosed and fixed this type of failure—into a reproducible procedure that any team member can follow, including someone paged at 2am without full context. For agentic AI pipelines, runbooks are especially important because the failure modes are more varied and less familiar than those of traditional software pipelines.
Why It Matters

Without structured triage, teams respond to all pipeline failures the same way: read the log, guess at the cause, try a fix. This works tolerably when the team is small and everyone knows the system. It fails as the team grows, as the pipeline acquires new failure modes, and as on-call shifts expand to cover people who did not write the eval harness. A flaky eval that should be auto-retried gets escalated to an engineer who spends an hour debugging a non-existent regression. A real eval regression that needs a prompt fix gets labeled 'infrastructure' and retried until the deadline, shipping a degraded agent.

Categorized runbooks with explicit decision criteria eliminate these misroutes. When the failure is categorized correctly in the first 5 minutes, the right person is engaged immediately, the correct remedy is applied, and mean time to resolve (MTTR) drops significantly. Over time, runbook data—which category fails most often, which takes longest to resolve—guides investment in prevention.
Benefits
Reduced MTTR — a responder following a runbook reaches the correct diagnosis in 5–10 minutes instead of 30–60 minutes of exploratory debugging.
Correct escalation routing — failures are routed to the model team, prompt team, infrastructure on-call, or security team based on category, not on whoever sees the Slack alert first.
Flaky eval isolation — auto-retry policies prevent flaky evals from blocking the pipeline on first occurrence while generating a ticket for root-cause investigation.
Institutional memory preservation — runbooks capture how senior engineers have resolved past failures, making the knowledge accessible to new team members without shadowing.
Prevention feedback loop — tracking failure categories over time identifies systemic problems (e.g., high rate of provider API failures) that warrant infrastructure investment.
Trade-offs
Runbook maintenance burden — runbooks become stale as the pipeline evolves; a runbook that references a step removed months ago actively misleads the responder during an incident.
Categorization overhead — adding explicit failure categories and exit codes to pipeline scripts requires upfront engineering work; the benefit materializes later when failures occur.
Auto-retry false negatives — auto-retrying flaky evals may occasionally retry a real regression and pass the second time, masking an actual quality problem; retry policies must be conservative.
Failure Taxonomy for Agentic AI Pipelines

Agentic AI pipeline failures fall into five categories. Each category has a distinct signal in the logs and a distinct resolution path. Using a structured taxonomy—communicated via pipeline exit codes and alert annotations—enables automated routing before a human is involved.

Eval regression — pass_rate dropped below the threshold or one or more previously-passing cases now fail; no API errors in the eval run logs. Cause: a code change, prompt change, or model configuration change degraded behavior. Owner: model team or prompt team.

Infrastructure failure — LLM provider API returned HTTP 5xx, connection timeout, or rate-limit errors that exceeded the retry budget; the eval run did not complete. Cause: provider outage, network issue, or insufficient rate limit quota. Owner: infrastructure team.

Schema mismatch — the eval scoring script fails because the dataset schema version does not match the script's expected schema; typically caused by a dataset version bump without a corresponding script update. Owner: data team or platform team.

Flaky eval — the pipeline fails on first run but passes on a second run with no code changes; the same case_id produces different pass/fail outcomes across runs. Cause: model temperature > 0, ambiguous expected output, or silent model provider updates. Owner: data team to fix case.
Code / lint / unit failure — the application code itself fails a linter, type checker, or unit test; this is not LLM-specific and is resolved by the application engineer who made the failing change.
Architecture: Triage Decision Tree

attrs.alt: Pipeline failure triage decision tree: categorization by stage, eval regression vs infra error disambiguation, flaky eval detection pattern, and runbook routing per category

content.text: Mental Model: The Emergency Room Triage Desk

An emergency room does not treat all patients immediately in arrival order. A triage nurse assesses severity and category first—chest pain goes to cardiac, a broken arm goes to orthopedics—so the right specialist is engaged immediately. A well-run CI failure triage process is the same: within 5 minutes of a pipeline failure, the failure category is determined, the correct 'specialist' (team or runbook) is engaged, and irrelevant teams are not disturbed. The triage decision tree is the triage nurse: it asks structured questions about the failure and routes it to the right runbook.
Distinguishing Flaky Evals from Real Regressions

The hardest triage decision is distinguishing a flaky eval (noise) from a real regression (signal). Both look like a failing pipeline. The primary discriminator is whether the failure is reproducible: re-run the failed job with no code changes. If it passes, the failure was flaky. If it fails again on the same case IDs, it is a real regression. A practical auto-retry policy: allow at most two automatic retries on eval failures where the error type is consistent with flakiness (high API error rate, same marginal case IDs, pass rate within 2% of threshold). After two retries without passing, treat it as a real failure and require human investigation.

Automated flaky detection tracks each case_id's historical pass/fail ratio. A case with a 70% historical pass rate at temperature=0 is definitionally flaky—its expected output is ambiguous. These cases should be quarantined into a non-blocking 'soft eval' suite until their expected output is tightened. Quarantine does not mean deletion: the case behavior is still valuable to observe; it just should not gate promotion.
When to Use Structured Triage
Use structured triage when the team has more than 2–3 engineers contributing to the same pipeline; informal 'whoever is around' routing does not scale.
Use it when the pipeline runs frequently enough (more than 20 times per week) that failures are a recurring operational burden, not rare events.
Use it when eval failures have caused production regressions in the past because the team could not quickly determine whether to retry or escalate.
Skip formal runbooks for single-engineer teams or early prototypes where the pipeline is simple and the engineer knows the system end to end.
How to Implement: Pipeline Exit Codes and Alert Routing
Define a structured failure taxonomy with named categories and corresponding exit codes (e.g., exit 2 = eval regression, exit 3 = infra error, exit 4 = schema mismatch, exit 5 = flaky eval).
Update the promotion gate and eval scripts to emit the correct exit code and a structured JSON summary to a known log file location.
Configure auto-retry in the CI workflow: on exit code 3 (infra error) or 5 (flaky), retry up to 2 times; on exit codes 2 and 4, do not retry and route to the appropriate team.
Write a runbook document for each failure category, stored in the repo under docs/runbooks/; link each runbook from the CI failure alert message.
Configure alert routing in your alerting system (PagerDuty, OpsGenie): exit code 2 pages the model/prompt team; exit code 3 pages infra on-call; exit code 4 pages the data team.
Review runbooks quarterly: after each post-mortem, update the relevant runbook to include the new diagnosis step or mitigation that was discovered during the incident.
Example: Structured Exit Codes in the Promotion Gate

# scripts/check_promotion_gate.py
# Exit codes:
#   0 = promotion gate PASSED
#   1 = unexpected / unclassified failure
#   2 = EVAL_REGRESSION (quality gate failed)
#   3 = INFRA_ERROR (API errors exceeded threshold)
#   4 = SCHEMA_MISMATCH (dataset schema incompatible)
#   5 = FLAKY_EVAL (marginal pass rate, high API error rate)

import json
import sys
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from pathlib import Path

EXIT_PASS = 0
EXIT_EVAL_REGRESSION = 2
EXIT_INFRA_ERROR = 3
EXIT_SCHEMA_MISMATCH = 4
EXIT_FLAKY_EVAL = 5

# Threshold below which we suspect flaky, not regression
FLAKY_MARGIN = 0.02  # within 2% of threshold

def classify_failure(results: dict, manifest: dict) -> tuple[int, str]:
    """Return (exit_code, reason) based on failure classification."""
    summary = results["summary"]
    rules = manifest["promotion_rules"]
    min_rate = rules["min_pass_rate"]

    api_error_rate = summary.get("api_error_rate", 0.0)
    pass_rate = summary.get("pass_rate", 0.0)
    schema_version = results.get("schema_version")
    expected_schema = manifest.get("schema_version")

    # Schema mismatch check first (script cannot produce valid results)
    if schema_version and expected_schema and schema_version != expected_schema:
        return EXIT_SCHEMA_MISMATCH, (
            f"Schema version mismatch: results have v{schema_version}, "
            f"manifest expects v{expected_schema}"
        )

    # High API error rate → infra problem, not a regression
    if api_error_rate > 0.10:  # >10% of calls failed
        return EXIT_INFRA_ERROR, (
            f"API error rate {api_error_rate:.1%} exceeds threshold 10%; "
            "likely provider outage or rate limit"
        )

    # Marginal fail near threshold + some API errors → suspect flaky
    if pass_rate < min_rate and pass_rate >= (min_rate - FLAKY_MARGIN) and api_error_rate > 0.01:
        return EXIT_FLAKY_EVAL, (
            f"pass_rate {pass_rate:.3f} is within flaky margin of threshold {min_rate} "
            f"with API error rate {api_error_rate:.1%}; re-run before escalating"
        )

    # Clear regression: pass rate below threshold, no infra noise
    if pass_rate < min_rate:
        failing_cases = [r["id"] for r in results.get("results", []) if not r["passed"]]
        return EXIT_EVAL_REGRESSION, (
            f"EVAL REGRESSION: pass_rate {pass_rate:.3f} < threshold {min_rate}. "
            f"Failing case IDs: {failing_cases[:10]}{'...' if len(failing_cases) > 10 else ''}"
        )

    # Check regression count
    regressions = summary.get("regression_count", 0)
    if regressions > rules.get("max_regression_count", 0):
        return EXIT_EVAL_REGRESSION, (
            f"EVAL REGRESSION: {regressions} previously-passing cases now fail"
        )

    return EXIT_PASS, f"PASSED: pass_rate={pass_rate:.3f}"

def main():
    import argparse
    import yaml

    parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
    parser.add_argument("--results", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--manifest", required=True)
    parser.add_argument("--failure-summary", default="failure_summary.json")
    args = parser.parse_args()

    with open(args.results) as f:
        results = json.load(f)
    with open(args.manifest) as f:
        manifest = yaml.safe_load(f)

    exit_code, reason = classify_failure(results, manifest)

    # Write structured failure summary for alert routing
    summary = {
        "exit_code": exit_code,
        "category": {0: "PASS", 2: "EVAL_REGRESSION", 3: "INFRA_ERROR",
                     4: "SCHEMA_MISMATCH", 5: "FLAKY_EVAL"}.get(exit_code, "UNKNOWN"),
        "reason": reason,
        "runbook": f"docs/runbooks/{exit_code}.md" if exit_code > 0 else None,
    }
    with open(args.failure_summary, "w") as f:
        json.dump(summary, f, indent=2)

    print(f"[{summary['category']}] {reason}")
    sys.exit(exit_code)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
Example: CI Auto-Retry Policy

# .github/workflows/eval-with-triage.yml
jobs:
  eval-harness:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      failure_category: ${{ steps.gate.outputs.category }}
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - run: pip install -e .[eval]

      - name: Run eval and gate
        id: gate
        continue-on-error: true  # let subsequent steps check exit code
        run: |
          python scripts/run_evals.py --output results/eval_results.json
          python scripts/check_promotion_gate.py \
            --results results/eval_results.json \
            --manifest evals/manifest.yaml \
            --failure-summary results/failure_summary.json
          echo "category=$(jq -r .category results/failure_summary.json)" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT

      - name: Load failure summary
        if: steps.gate.outcome == 'failure'
        run: cat results/failure_summary.json

      - name: Auto-retry on infra or flaky
        if: |
          steps.gate.outcome == 'failure' &&
          (steps.gate.outputs.category == 'INFRA_ERROR' ||
           steps.gate.outputs.category == 'FLAKY_EVAL')
        uses: nick-fields/retry@v2
        with:
          timeout_minutes: 30
          max_attempts: 2
          command: |
            python scripts/run_evals.py --output results/eval_results_retry.json
            python scripts/check_promotion_gate.py \
              --results results/eval_results_retry.json \
              --manifest evals/manifest.yaml

      - name: Route alert on eval regression
        if: steps.gate.outputs.category == 'EVAL_REGRESSION'
        run: |
          python scripts/route_alert.py \
            --category EVAL_REGRESSION \
            --summary results/failure_summary.json \
            --channel "#model-team-alerts"

      - name: Route alert on schema mismatch
        if: steps.gate.outputs.category == 'SCHEMA_MISMATCH'
        run: |
          python scripts/route_alert.py \
            --category SCHEMA_MISMATCH \
            --channel "#data-team-alerts"

      - name: Fail if not pass
        if: steps.gate.outcome == 'failure'
        run: |
          echo "Pipeline failed with category: ${{ steps.gate.outputs.category }}"
          exit 1

      - uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        if: always()
        with:
          name: eval-triage-results
          path: results/
Sample Runbook: Eval Regression

# Runbook: Eval Regression (exit code 2)

## Trigger
CI pipeline failed with category EVAL_REGRESSION.
pass_rate fell below threshold, or previously-passing cases now fail.

## Initial Triage (< 5 minutes)
1. Open the CI run → download artifact `eval-triage-results/failure_summary.json`.
2. Note the failing case IDs listed in `reason` field.
3. Check `results/eval_results.json` for those IDs; compare `output` vs `expected`.
4. Identify the most recent commit that changed prompt files or model config.

## Diagnosis Branches

### If prompt changed in this PR:
- Assign to prompt team.
- Steps: review system prompt diff, reproduce failure locally with failing case IDs,
  fix prompt, push to PR branch, re-run CI.

### If model config changed (model_id or temperature):
- Assign to model team.
- Steps: revert model_id to previous version, confirm CI passes, investigate
  whether new model requires prompt adjustments before re-attempting upgrade.

### If no prompt or model change in this PR:
- Suspect dataset version bump introduced harder cases.
- Check evals repo for recent dataset changes that coincide with the failure.
- Assign to data team to review new case expected outputs.

## Escalation
- If failure cannot be attributed to a recent change after 30 minutes: page
  model team lead via PagerDuty.
- Do NOT lower the promotion threshold to unblock the PR.

## Resolution Verification
- Re-run CI on fixed PR; confirm pass_rate >= threshold and regression_count == 0.
- Close the related GitHub issue with a note on root cause.
Real-World Example: Multi-Category Failure Week

During a week of heavy development, a team's agentic AI pipeline fails four times. Without triage infrastructure, all four failures look identical in Slack: 'CI failed.' With structured exit codes, the failures route correctly. First failure: exit code 3 (INFRA_ERROR). The OpenAI API returned 503 errors for 15 minutes. Auto-retry triggered; the second run 8 minutes later passed. No human was paged. Second failure: exit code 2 (EVAL_REGRESSION). A prompt engineer's PR updated the system prompt and 8 previously-passing safety cases now fail. The alert routes to #model-team-alerts. The engineer identifies the problematic instruction, reverts it, and reruns CI successfully.

Third failure: exit code 4 (SCHEMA_MISMATCH). The data team pushed dataset v2.4.0 with a new required field, but the scoring script was not updated. The alert routes to #data-team-alerts. The data team updates the script and the dataset minor version in the pipeline config. Fourth failure: exit code 5 (FLAKY_EVAL). Case ID routing-case-0847 has been producing inconsistent results for three days. Auto-retry passes. A ticket is filed to quarantine the case. The data team investigates and discovers the expected output accepts one of two equivalent phrasings; they update the judge rubric. All four failures were resolved by the correct team within 30 minutes because the triage system told them exactly what to do.
Alert Routing and On-Call Escalation

Alert routing maps failure category to team. Eval regressions route to the model or prompt team's Slack channel and optionally page the team lead if not acknowledged within 2 hours. Infrastructure errors route to the infra on-call. Schema mismatches route to the data platform team. Flaky eval tickets are filed automatically in the backlog—not paged—with the case IDs and historical pass rates attached for investigation.

Production failures (staging or production deploy stage) always page the on-call engineer immediately regardless of category, because production impact may be live. The on-call engineer's first action is always to check whether a rollback is needed; only after confirming or executing the rollback do they proceed with root-cause investigation using the runbook.
Common Mistakes
Treating all eval failures as regressions — retrying a real regression multiple times does not fix it and delays the investigation; the failure category must be established first.
Stale runbooks — a runbook that references a script that was renamed or a step that was removed actively misleads the responder; runbooks must be updated with the pipeline.

No structured failure summary — if the triage information is buried in 500 lines of CI log output, responders will not read it during an incident; write the failure category to a structured JSON file and surface it prominently in the alert.

Lowering thresholds instead of fixing regressions — when a real regression is misclassified as flaky and auto-retried into a pass, or when the threshold is lowered to unblock a PR, the degraded behavior ships to production.

Missing the auto-retry limit — auto-retrying indefinitely on flaky evals wastes CI minutes and may mask a pattern of worsening flakiness; cap retries at 2 and file a ticket on any exceeded retry limit.
Best Practices
Define failure categories and exit codes before the first production incident, not during one; the taxonomy is clearest before tribal knowledge hardens into muscle memory.
Store runbooks in the same repository as the pipeline that they cover; they should be versioned alongside the pipeline code and referenced in CI job summaries via URL.
Conduct quarterly runbook fire drills: have an engineer who did not write the runbook follow it against a staging pipeline failure to identify gaps before a real incident.
Track failure category frequency in a dashboard; a sustained increase in INFRA_ERROR failures signals a rate-limit or reliability problem that needs an SLA conversation with the provider.
For each post-mortem, identify which runbook step was missing or misleading and update the runbook the same day; stale runbooks compound during future incidents.
Performance and Scalability

Triage logic adds minimal overhead—a Python script reading a JSON file and emitting an exit code runs in under one second. The meaningful performance investment is in the auto-retry logic: a retry that spins up a full eval harness run adds 5–20 minutes to the pipeline on failure, which is acceptable for infra errors (rare) but should be avoided for eval regressions (where retrying does not help). The classification logic must be accurate enough that INFRA_ERROR and FLAKY_EVAL categories only trigger when the failure is genuinely in those categories, not when a real regression happens to coincide with a single API error.
Tooling and Ecosystem
PagerDuty / OpsGenie — industry-standard on-call paging tools; CI scripts can POST to their APIs with failure category metadata to route alerts to the correct team's escalation policy.
GitHub Actions job summaries — use echo '## Triage Summary' >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY to surface the failure category directly in the Actions UI, reducing the need to dig through logs.

nick-fields/retry GitHub Action — a community action for retrying CI steps with configurable attempt counts and delays; useful for auto-retrying eval harness jobs without writing custom retry shell logic.

Datadog / Grafana — monitoring platforms that can ingest failure category metrics from CI artifacts and plot failure frequency by category over time, enabling trend analysis and prevention investment decisions.
Comparison with Alternatives
Triage Approach
Categorization
Alert Routing
Best for
Ad hoc (read logs)
None
Manual
Solo or very early stage
Exit codes + runbooks (this pattern)
Structured
Automated
Most teams; low overhead, high impact
AIOps / ML-based failure classification
ML model
Automated
Large orgs with sufficient failure volume for training data
Managed eval platforms (Braintrust)
Built-in
Platform UI + hooks
Teams wanting managed eval infrastructure
Security and Reliability
Failure summary JSON files may contain case IDs, model outputs, and partial prompts; treat them as potentially sensitive and restrict artifact access to authorized team members.
Alert webhook tokens (PagerDuty API key, Slack webhook URL) must be stored as CI secrets, not hardcoded in the alert routing script.
The auto-retry policy must have a hard cap—never retry more than N times regardless of category—to prevent a misconfigured pipeline from looping indefinitely and incurring unbounded LLM API spend.

Runbooks should document which actions require additional approvals (e.g., reverting a production deploy requires two on-call engineers to confirm), reducing the risk of a panicked solo action during an incident.
Key Takeaways
Agentic AI pipelines have at least five distinct failure categories: eval regression, infra error, schema mismatch, flaky eval, and code error; each requires a different response.
Structured exit codes are the foundation of automated triage; the promotion gate script must emit a machine-readable failure category, not just a non-zero exit.
Auto-retry only infra errors and flaky evals; never auto-retry eval regressions or schema mismatches, as retrying does not resolve them.
The primary discriminator between flaky and regression is reproducibility on re-run with identical configuration; a failure that passes on re-run is flaky until proven otherwise.
Runbooks must be stored in the repo alongside the pipeline, linked from alert messages, and updated after every post-mortem to remain useful.
Alert routing must be automated: the CI pipeline should post the failure category and runbook link to the correct team's channel without requiring a human to read logs first.

Track failure category frequency over time; a rising INFRA_ERROR rate signals a provider reliability problem, while rising FLAKY_EVAL rates signal a dataset quality problem, both of which have systemic fixes.
